policy_statement: TMG will promote Tokyo-Style Smart Agriculture to maintain stable agricultural production under climate change and realize high profits on a small scale using IoT, AI, and other advanced technologies, including R&D across three fields.
mechanism_description: TMG will promote IoT/AI-enabled smart agriculture and related R&D to help keep agricultural production stable under climate-change stresses such as typhoons and extreme heat.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard (heat/typhoons) → smart/precision management → ↓ sensitivity & ↓ disruption → ↓ yield loss / ↑ production stability
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: Farmers and agricultural managers adopt IoT/AI-enabled monitoring, decision-support, automation, and climate-resilient production practices (often developed/validated via R&D).  
2) Mediating physical/ecological process: Improved sensing and control of microclimate, water, nutrients, pests/disease, and operations enables quicker, targeted responses to heat stress, heavy rainfall, and storm impacts (e.g., optimized irrigation, protected cultivation, early warning/response).  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced vulnerability of agriculture to climate extremes, resulting in more stable yields and fewer climate-related production shocks.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced climate-hazard-driven yield loss and farm disruption, maintaining agricultural production under extreme heat/typhoons.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Climate hazards impact agriculture; smart-ag technologies reduce sensitivity/exposure and speed recovery, thereby stabilizing production—an adaptation pathway.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While smart agriculture can incidentally reduce inputs (fertilizer/water/energy) and emissions, the stated objective and most generalizable effect across policies using this mechanism is resilience/stability under climate stress.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a specific smart-ag policy is explicitly designed mainly for emissions reductions (e.g., nitrous oxide reduction via precision fertilizer) it could be mitigation/resource-efficiency at the policy level, but that is not the dominant mechanism here.  
4) Applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: “Technology adoption → climate-resilient agricultural production” is inherently about maintaining function under climate stress; thus Adaptation is the consistent primary category.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment is deferred: Any mitigation/resource-efficiency co-benefits (reduced fertilizer, energy, methane) require policy-specific evidence and are not used to set the primary category here.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Smart/precision agriculture can also drive mitigation/resource-efficiency outcomes (reduced fertilizer-related N₂O, energy savings, improved manure management) depending on design; however, this canonical mechanism is explicitly framed around resilience and production stability under climate extremes, so Adaptation remains primary.
key_indicators: “promote Tokyo-Style Smart Agriculture”; “To maintain stable agricultural production even under climate change”; “including large typhoons and extreme heat”; “making use of IoT, AI”; “move ahead with research and development”
co_benefits: Higher profitability/productivity (“realize high profits on a small scale”); technology/innovation spillovers and digitalization of farming
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be framed as a broader sectoral strategy/program (planning-strategy) because it is phrased as “promote” plus R&D, but absent explicit plans/roadmaps and given the emphasis on advancing R&D and deployment, classed as an enabling incentive/programmatic support instrument.
